title: Countable & Uncountable Nouns
subtitle: Mass, count, and the quantifiers that police them
intro: Some nouns can be pluralised and counted (a policy, two policies). Others are treated as a single mass (information, research, advice). The distinction is not logical — it is grammatical convention — and it controls which quantifiers, articles and verb forms are legal. A confident Band 6.5+ writer never writes 'informations', 'many research', or 'advices'.

rules.heading: Common IELTS uncountables
rules.body: information, research, evidence, knowledge, advice, equipment, machinery, furniture, traffic, pollution, progress, behaviour. All take singular verbs and no -s.

rules.heading: Count-only quantifiers
rules.body: many / (a) few / several / a number of / every → countables only: 'many students', 'a few errors'.

rules.heading: Uncount-only quantifiers
rules.body: much / (a) little / a great deal of / a large amount of → uncountables only: 'much research', 'a great deal of progress'.

rules.heading: Quantifiers that work with both
rules.body: some, any, plenty of, lots of, most, all, a lot of: 'a lot of students' and 'a lot of information' are both fine.

examples.sentence: Recent research suggests that a small amount of daily exercise yields substantial health benefits.
examples.note: 'Research' uncountable → singular verb and 'a small amount of'.

examples.sentence: Policymakers have introduced several measures to curb traffic congestion.
The chart provides information on the number of international students in four countries.
Young people tend to give too much weight to online advice.
There is ample evidence that air pollution accelerates cognitive decline.

band8_marker: Band 8 writers use hybrid countability well: 'a body of evidence', 'a piece of advice', 'an item of clothing', 'three pieces of luggage'. These unitisers promote an uncountable noun into an idea the reader can enumerate — a hallmark of academic register.

common_errors.fix: These are uncountable → 'much information', 'a piece of advice', 'research' (no -s).
'A great deal of' is for uncountables → 'a large number of students'.

practice.title: Count or not?
practice.instruction: Click the error in each sentence.

items.prompt: The report contains [A:useful informations] about [B:recent trends].
[A:Many research] has been published [B:on this issue].
She gave me [A:a very good advice] about [B:my application].
items.explanation: → 'a very good piece of advice' or 'very good advice'.

items.prompt: The [A:furnitures] in the office [B:need] replacing.
[A:Plenty of] students complained about [B:the few equipment] available.
Traffic [A:are] becoming [B:a major concern] in most capitals.
We [A:made] significant [B:progresses] this quarter.
[A:Few] pollution [B:can be eliminated] without structural change.

practice.title: Choose the quantifier
practice.instruction: Fill in with many / much / few / little / a number of / an amount of.

items.prompt: Only ___ progress has been made on reducing emissions.
___ students struggle with the IELTS Writing task.
The survey generated ___ valuable information.
items.answer: a great deal of / a lot of

items.prompt: Very ___ evidence supports that claim.
He gave me ___ pieces of advice before my interview.
There are ___ reasons to reconsider the policy.
After the storm, there was ___ damage to the harbour.
We received ___ feedback from participants.
items.answer: a great deal of / a lot of

items.prompt: There is ___ that remote work increases productivity.
The researcher interviewed ___ for the study.
The office has ___ in the storage room.
___ people attended the protest.
Her report provided ___ we needed.
There has been ___ this year compared to last.
I have ___ for the task.
Only ___ were aware of the change.
